page: Vultr voc-c-16c-32gb-300s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute server configured with 16 vCPUs and 32.0 GB of RAM. Powered by an AMD EPYC-Rome processor operating at 2.0 GHz on the x86_64 architecture, this instance features 16 physical cores with a shared CPU allocation. The hardware profile includes a 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio, providing 2.0 GB of memory per core, alongside 300 GB of local NVMe SSD storage.

Performance benchmarks position this server as a capable option for multi-threaded workloads. The AMD EPYC processor delivers strong single-core CPU performance in stress-ng tests, placing in the top 25%. Multi-core CPU performance, PassMark CPU scores, and memory bandwidth tests all register in the average tier. Specifically, the system achieves a static web serving throughput of 16.16 GB/sec and handles 30.51 million Redis SET operations per second, indicating reliable execution for standard transactional and web serving tasks.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the shared CPU allocation allows Vultr to offer a high vCPU and memory density at a lower resource tier. However, the shared nature of the CPU means compute performance may experience variability depending on host node neighbor activity, which is a notable tradeoff compared to dedicated-core instances. This server is well-suited for workloads such as web serving, multi-threaded decompression tasks, and moderate database operations that benefit from high core counts and fast NVMe storage without requiring sustained, isolated CPU performance.

Benchmark results show strong single-core CPU performance, while multi-core performance, memory bandwidth, and database operations fall within the average range. The server achieves a static web serving throughput of 16.16 GB/sec and handles 30.51 million Redis SET operations per second. Due to its shared CPU allocation, it offers a cost-effective option for resource-dense workloads, though performance may vary based on host node activity. It is best suited for web serving, multi-threaded decompression, and moderate database operations.
